title: Exodus 1
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Exodus
paragraphs: 1 These are the names of the sons of Israel who went to Egypt with Jacob. Each man came with his family:
2 Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah;
3 Issachar, Zebulun, and Benjamin;
4 Dan and Naphtali; Gad and Asher.
5 The family members of Jacob were seventy in all. Joseph was already in Egypt.
6 Now Joseph and all his brothers and all that generation died.
7 But the Israelites were exceedingly fruitful. They multiplied greatly, increased in numbers, and became so numerous that the land was filled with them.
8 Then a new king, to whom Joseph meant nothing, came to power in Egypt.
9 "Look," he said to his people, "the Israelites have become too many and too strong for us."
10 "Come, let us deal with them in a clever way. If we don't, they will grow even more. If a war breaks out, they may join our enemies. They could fight against us and leave the country."
11 So the Egyptians put slave drivers over the Israelites to oppress them with forced labor. The Israelites built Pithom and Rameses as supply cities for Pharaoh.
12 But the more they were oppressed, the more they multiplied and spread. So the Egyptians came to dread the Israelites.
13 The Egyptians worked the Israelites without mercy.
14 They made their lives bitter with hard work in brick and mortar. They also made them do all kinds of work in the fields. Every job they gave was harsh.
15 Then the king of Egypt spoke to the Hebrew midwives. Their names were Shiphrah and Puah.
16 He said, "When you help the Hebrew women give birth, watch them on the birth stools. If the child is a son, kill him. But if it is a daughter, let her live."
17 But the midwives feared God. They did not do what the king of Egypt had told them to do. They let the boys live.
18 So the king of Egypt called the midwives. He asked them, "Why have you done this? Why have you let the boys live?"
19 The midwives answered Pharaoh, "The Hebrew women are not like the Egyptian women. They are strong, and they give birth before a midwife arrives."
20 So God was good to the midwives. And the people multiplied and became even more numerous.
21 Because the midwives feared God, He gave them families of their own.
22 Then Pharaoh gave this order to all his people: "Every son born to the Hebrews you must throw into the Nile. But every daughter you may let live."

title: Exodus 2
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Exodus
paragraphs: 1 A man from the family of Levi married a daughter of Levi.
2 She became pregnant and gave birth to a son. She saw that he was a beautiful child. So she hid him for three months.

3 But soon she could not hide him any longer. She got a basket made of reeds. She covered it with sticky tar to keep water out. Then she put the child in the basket. She set it among the tall grass by the edge of the Nile.
4 His sister stood far away to watch what would happen to him.
5 Soon Pharaoh's daughter came down to bathe in the Nile. Her servants were walking along the riverbank. She saw the basket among the tall grass. She sent her servant girl to go and get it.
6 When she opened it, she saw the child. The little boy was crying. She felt sorry for him. She said, "This is one of the Hebrew children."
7 Then his sister said to Pharaoh's daughter, "Shall I go and call a Hebrew woman to feed and care for the baby for you?"
8 "Go ahead," Pharaoh's daughter told her. So the girl went and called the boy's mother.
9 Pharaoh's daughter said to her, "Take this child and care for him for me. I will pay you." So the woman took the boy and cared for him.
10 When the child was older, she brought him to Pharaoh's daughter. He became her son. She named him Moses. She said, "I pulled him out of the water."
11 One day, after Moses had grown up, he went out to his own people. He watched their hard work. He saw an Egyptian beating a Hebrew, one of his own people.
12 Moses looked all around and saw no one. So he killed the Egyptian and hid his body in the sand.
13 The next day Moses went out again. He saw two Hebrews fighting. He asked the one who was wrong, "Why are you hitting your fellow Hebrew?"
14 But the man replied, "Who made you our leader and judge? Are you planning to kill me, like you killed the Egyptian?" Then Moses was afraid. He thought, "People know what I have done."
15 When Pharaoh heard about this, he tried to kill Moses. But Moses ran away from Pharaoh. He lived in the land of Midian. He sat down beside a well.
16 The priest of Midian had seven daughters. They came to get water and fill the water tanks for their father's sheep.
17 But some shepherds came and drove them away. Moses stood up to help them. He gave water to their sheep.
18 The daughters returned to their father Reuel. He asked them, "Why have you come back so early today?"
19 They replied, "An Egyptian saved us from the shepherds. He even drew water for us and gave it to the sheep."
20 "So where is he?" their father asked. "Why did you leave the man behind? Invite him to come and eat with us."
21 Moses agreed to stay with the man. The man gave his daughter Zipporah to Moses as his wife.
22 She gave birth to a son. Moses named him Gershom. He said, "I have become a stranger in a strange land."
23 A long time passed. The king of Egypt died. The Israelites cried out in pain under their hard slavery. Their cry for help went up to God.
24 God heard their cries. He remembered his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.
25 God saw the Israelites. He cared about them.

title: Exodus 3
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Exodus
paragraphs: 1 Moses was looking after the flock of his father-in-law Jethro. Jethro was the priest of Midian. Moses led the flock to the far side of the desert. He came to Horeb, the mountain of God.
2 There the angel of the LORD appeared to him in flames of fire from within a bush. Moses saw the bush on fire. But it did not burn up.
3 So Moses thought, "I must go over and see this strange sight. Why is the bush not burning up?"
4 The LORD saw that Moses had come over to look. God called to him from within the bush, "Moses, Moses!" "Here I am," he answered.
5 "Do not come any closer," God said. "Take off your sandals, for the place where you are standing is holy ground."
6 Then he said, "I am the God of your father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac and the God of Jacob." At this, Moses hid his face. He was afraid to look at God.
7 The LORD said, "I have indeed seen the misery of my people in Egypt. I have heard them crying out because of the people who hurt them. I am concerned about their suffering.

8 So I have come down to rescue them from the hand of the Egyptians. I will bring them up out of that land into a good and spacious land, a land flowing with milk and honey. It is the home of the Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites.
9 The cry of the Israelites has reached me. I have seen how badly the Egyptians treat them.
10 So go! I am sending you to Pharaoh. Bring my people the Israelites out of Egypt."
11 But Moses asked God, "Who am I? How can I go to Pharaoh and bring the Israelites out of Egypt?"
12 "I will surely be with you," God said. "And this will be the sign that I have sent you. When you have brought the people out of Egypt, you will all worship God on this mountain."
13 Then Moses asked God, "Suppose I go to the Israelites. I will say to them, 'The God of your fathers has sent me to you.' Then they will ask me, 'What is his name?' What should I tell them?"
14 God said to Moses, "I AM WHO I AM. This is what you are to say to the Israelites: 'I AM has sent me to you.'"

15 God also told Moses, "Say to the Israelites, 'The LORD, the God of your fathers — the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac and the God of Jacob — has sent me to you. This is my name forever, the name you shall call me from generation to generation.'

16 Go, gather the elders of Israel. Say to them, 'The LORD, the God of your fathers — the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — has appeared to me. He said: I have surely watched over you. I have seen what has been done to you in Egypt.

17 And I have promised this: I will bring you up out of your pain in Egypt. I will lead you into the land of the Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites — a land flowing with milk and honey.'

18 The elders of Israel will listen to you. Then you must go with them to the king of Egypt. Tell him, 'The LORD, the God of the Hebrews, has met with us. Now please let us take a three-day journey into the desert. We want to offer sacrifices to the LORD our God.'
19 But I know that the king of Egypt will not let you go. Only a mighty hand will force him.
20 So I will stretch out my hand. I will strike the Egyptians with all the wonders I will do among them. After that, he will let you go.
21 And I will make the Egyptians kind to my people. When you leave, you will not go away with empty hands.

22 Every woman shall ask her neighbor for silver and gold jewelry and clothing. She shall ask any woman staying in her house. You will put them on your sons and daughters. So you will take riches from the Egyptians."

title: Exodus 4
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Exodus
paragraphs: 1 Then Moses answered, "What if they do not believe me? What if they will not listen to my voice? They may say, 'The LORD has not appeared to you.'"
2 And the LORD asked him, "What is that in your hand?" "A staff," he replied.
3 "Throw it on the ground," said the LORD. So Moses threw it on the ground. It became a snake, and he ran from it.
4 "Stretch out your hand and grab it by the tail," the LORD said. Moses reached out his hand and caught the snake. It turned back into a staff in his hand.
5 "This sign is so that they may believe. They will know that the LORD, the God of their fathers, has appeared to you — the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob."
6 Then the LORD said to Moses, "Put your hand inside your cloak." So he put his hand inside his cloak. When he took it out, his hand was leprous, white as snow.
7 "Put your hand back inside your cloak," said the LORD. So Moses put his hand back inside. When he took it out, it was healthy again, like the rest of his skin.
8 And the LORD said, "If they will not believe you or the first sign, they may believe the second sign.

9 But what if they still do not believe these two signs? What if they will not listen to your voice? Then take some water from the Nile. Pour it on the dry ground. The water from the Nile will become blood on the ground."
10 "Please, Lord," Moses replied, "I have never been good with words. I was not good before, and I am not now. I am slow of speech and tongue."
11 And the LORD said to him, "Who gave human beings their mouths? Who makes a person able to speak or not to speak? Who makes them see or be blind? Is it not I, the LORD?
12 Now go! I will help you as you speak. I will teach you what to say."
13 But Moses replied, "Please, Lord, send someone else."

14 Then the anger of the LORD burned against Moses. He said, "What about your brother Aaron the Levite? I know that he can speak well. He is now on his way to meet you. When he sees you, he will be glad in his heart.
15 You must speak to him. Put the words in his mouth. I will help both of you to speak. I will teach you what to do.
16 He will speak to the people for you. He will be your spokesman. And you will be like God to him.
17 But take this staff in your hand. You will perform signs with it."
18 Then Moses went back to his father-in-law Jethro. He said, "Please let me return to my brothers in Egypt. I want to see if they are still alive." "Go in peace," Jethro replied.
19 Now the LORD had said to Moses in Midian, "Go back to Egypt. All the men who wanted to kill you are dead."
20 So Moses took his wife and sons. He put them on a donkey and headed back to Egypt. He also took the staff of God in his hand.
21 The LORD said to Moses, "When you go back to Egypt, perform all the wonders before Pharaoh. I have given you power to do them. But I will make his heart hard. He will not let the people go.
22 Then tell Pharaoh that this is what the LORD says: 'Israel is my firstborn son.
23 I told you to let my son go so that he may worship me. But you have refused to let him go. So I will kill your firstborn son!'"
24 Now at a lodging place along the way, the LORD met Moses. He was about to kill him.
25 But Zipporah took a sharp stone knife. She cut off her son's foreskin and touched it to Moses' feet. "Surely you are a bridegroom of blood to me," she said.
26 So the LORD let him alone. (When she said "bridegroom of blood," she was speaking of the circumcision.)
27 Meanwhile, the LORD had said to Aaron, "Go and meet Moses in the wilderness." So he went and met Moses at the mountain of God, and kissed him.
28 And Moses told Aaron everything the LORD had sent him to say. He also told him all the signs the LORD had commanded him to perform.
29 Then Moses and Aaron went and gathered all the elders of the Israelites.
30 Aaron told them everything the LORD had said to Moses. And Moses performed the signs before the people.
31 And they believed. They heard that the LORD had cared about the Israelites. They heard that he had seen their suffering. So they bowed down and worshiped.

title: Exodus 5
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Exodus
paragraphs: 1 After that, Moses and Aaron went to Pharaoh. They said, "This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: 'Let my people go. They must hold a festival for me in the desert.'"
2 But Pharaoh replied, "Who is the LORD that I should obey him? Why should I let Israel go? I do not know the LORD. And I will not let Israel go."

3 They answered, "The God of the Hebrews has met with us. Please let us take a three-day journey into the desert. We must offer sacrifices to the LORD our God. If not, he may send sickness on us or kill us with the sword."
4 But the king of Egypt said to them, "Moses and Aaron, why do you pull the people away from their work? Get back to your labor!"
5 Pharaoh also said, "Look, the people of the land are now many. And you would stop them from their work."
6 That same day Pharaoh gave orders to the slave masters and the foremen of the people. He said:
7 "Do not give the people straw for making bricks anymore. They must go and gather their own straw.
8 But they must make the same number of bricks as before. Do not reduce it. They are lazy. That is why they cry out, 'Let us go and sacrifice to our God.'
9 Make the work harder on the men. Then they will be busy and pay no attention to these lies."
10 So the slave masters and foremen went out to the people. They said, "This is what Pharaoh says: 'I will not give you straw anymore.
11 Go and get your own straw wherever you can find it. But your work will not be reduced at all.'"
12 So the people scattered all over the land of Egypt. They gathered dry grass for straw.
13 The slave masters kept pressing them. They said, "Make the same number of bricks each day, just as you did when straw was given."

14 Pharaoh's slave masters had set Israelite foremen over the people. Now these foremen were beaten. They were asked, "Why have you not made the full number of bricks yesterday or today, as you did before?"
15 So the Israelite foremen went and begged Pharaoh. They said, "Why are you treating your servants this way?
16 No straw is given to your servants. Yet we are told, 'Make bricks!' Look, your servants are being beaten. But the fault is with your own people."
17 "You are lazy people!" Pharaoh replied. "Lazy people! That is why you keep saying, 'Let us go and sacrifice to the LORD.'
18 Now get to work. You will be given no straw. Yet you must make the full number of bricks."
19 The Israelite foremen realized they were in trouble. They were told, "You must not reduce your daily number of bricks."
20 When they left Pharaoh, they met Moses and Aaron. The two were standing there, waiting for them.
21 The foremen said, "May the LORD look at you and judge you. You have made us a bad smell to Pharaoh and his officials. You have put a sword in their hand to kill us!"
22 So Moses returned to the LORD. He asked, "Lord, why have you brought trouble on this people? Is this why you sent me?
23 Ever since I went to Pharaoh to speak in your name, he has brought trouble on this people. And you have not rescued your people in any way."

title: Exodus 6
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Exodus
paragraphs: 1 But the LORD said to Moses, "Now you will see what I will do to Pharaoh. Because of my mighty hand, he will let the people go. Because of my strong hand, he will drive them out of his land."
2 God also told Moses, "I am the LORD.
3 I appeared to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob as God Almighty. But by my name the LORD I did not make myself fully known to them.
4 I also made my covenant with them. I promised to give them the land of Canaan, where they lived as foreigners.
5 I have also heard the deep cries of the Israelites. The Egyptians are making them slaves. And I have remembered my covenant.

6 So tell the Israelites: 'I am the LORD. I will bring you out from under the heavy load of the Egyptians. I will free you from their slavery. I will redeem you with an outstretched arm and with great acts of judgment.
7 I will take you as my own people, and I will be your God. Then you will know that I am the LORD your God. I am the one who brought you out from under the heavy load of the Egyptians.
8 And I will bring you into the land that I promised to give to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. I will give it to you as your own land. I am the LORD!'"
9 Moses told this message to the Israelites. But they did not listen to him. Their spirit was crushed by cruel slavery.
10 So the LORD said to Moses,
11 "Go and tell Pharaoh king of Egypt to let the Israelites go out of his land."
12 But Moses replied to the LORD, "The Israelites will not listen to me. So why would Pharaoh listen to me? I have uncircumcised lips."
13 Then the LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron. He gave them orders about the Israelites and Pharaoh king of Egypt. They were to bring the Israelites out of the land of Egypt.
14 These were the heads of their family groups: The sons of Reuben, the firstborn of Israel, were Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi. These were the family groups of Reuben.
15 The sons of Simeon were Jemuel, Jamin, Ohad, Jachin, Zohar, and Shaul. Shaul was the son of a Canaanite woman. These were the family groups of Simeon.
16 These were the names of the sons of Levi by their family records: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. Levi lived 137 years.
17 The sons of Gershon were Libni and Shimei, by their family groups.
18 The sons of Kohath were Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel. Kohath lived 133 years.
19 The sons of Merari were Mahli and Mushi. These were the family groups of the Levites by their family records.
20 Amram married his father's sister Jochebed. She bore him Aaron and Moses. Amram lived 137 years.
21 The sons of Izhar were Korah, Nepheg, and Zichri.
22 The sons of Uzziel were Mishael, Elzaphan, and Sithri.
23 Aaron married Elisheba, the daughter of Amminadab and sister of Nahshon. She bore him Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar.
24 The sons of Korah were Assir, Elkanah, and Abiasaph. These were the family groups of the Korahites.
25 Aaron's son Eleazar married one of the daughters of Putiel. She bore him Phinehas. These were the heads of the Levite families by their family groups.
26 This is the same Aaron and Moses to whom the LORD said, "Bring the Israelites out of the land of Egypt by their groups."
27 Moses and Aaron were the ones who spoke to Pharaoh king of Egypt. They worked to bring the Israelites out of Egypt.
28 Now on the day the LORD spoke to Moses in Egypt,
29 he said to him, "I am the LORD. Tell Pharaoh king of Egypt everything I say to you."
30 But Moses replied to the LORD, "I have uncircumcised lips. So why would Pharaoh listen to me?"

title: Exodus 7
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Exodus
paragraphs: 1 The LORD answered Moses, "Look, I have made you like God to Pharaoh. Your brother Aaron will be your prophet.
2 You must speak everything I command you. Your brother Aaron will tell Pharaoh to let the Israelites leave his land.
3 But I will make Pharaoh's heart hard. I will do many signs and wonders in the land of Egypt.
4 Pharaoh will not listen to you. Then I will lay my hand on Egypt. By powerful acts of judgment, I will bring my people the Israelites out of Egypt.
5 Then the Egyptians will know that I am the LORD. They will know when I reach out my hand against Egypt and bring the Israelites out."
6 So Moses and Aaron did just as the LORD had commanded them.
7 Moses was eighty years old. Aaron was eighty-three. That was when they spoke to Pharaoh.
8 The LORD said to Moses and Aaron,
9 "Pharaoh will tell you, 'Do a miracle.' Then say to Aaron, 'Take your staff and throw it down in front of Pharaoh.' It will become a snake."
10 So Moses and Aaron went to Pharaoh. They did just as the LORD had commanded. Aaron threw his staff down before Pharaoh and his officials. It became a snake.
11 But Pharaoh called the wise men, sorcerers, and magicians of Egypt. They also did the same things by their magic.
12 Each one threw down his staff, and it became a snake. But Aaron's staff swallowed up their staffs.
13 Still, Pharaoh's heart was hardened. He would not listen to them, just as the LORD had said.
14 Then the LORD said to Moses, "Pharaoh's heart is stubborn. He refuses to let the people go.
15 Go to Pharaoh in the morning. Watch as he walks out to the water. Wait on the bank of the Nile to meet him. Take in your hand the staff that became a snake.
16 Then say to him, 'The LORD, the God of the Hebrews, has sent me to tell you: Let my people go. They must worship me in the desert. But you have not listened until now.
17 This is what the LORD says: By this you will know that I am the LORD. Look — with the staff in my hand, I will hit the water of the Nile. It will turn to blood.
18 The fish in the Nile will die. The river will smell bad. The Egyptians will not be able to drink its water.'"

19 The LORD said to Moses, "Tell Aaron, 'Take your staff. Reach out your hand over the waters of Egypt — over their rivers, streams, ponds, and pools. They will become blood.' There will be blood all over the land of Egypt, even in the jars of wood and stone."
20 Moses and Aaron did just as the LORD had commanded. In front of Pharaoh and his officials, Aaron raised the staff. He hit the water of the Nile. All the water turned to blood.
21 The fish in the Nile died. The river smelled so bad that the Egyptians could not drink its water. There was blood all over the land of Egypt.
22 But the magicians of Egypt did the same things by their magic. So Pharaoh's heart was hardened. He would not listen to Moses and Aaron, just as the LORD had said.
23 Instead, Pharaoh turned around and went into his palace. He did not care about any of this.
24 So all the Egyptians dug around the Nile for water to drink. They could not drink the water from the river.
25 Seven full days passed after the LORD had struck the Nile.

title: Exodus 8
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Exodus
paragraphs: 1 Then the LORD said to Moses, "Go to Pharaoh. Tell him this is what the LORD says: 'Let my people go, so they can worship me.
2 If you refuse to let them go, I will hit your whole country with frogs.

3 The Nile River will be full of frogs. They will come into your palace and into your bedroom. They will jump onto your bed. They will go into the houses of your officials and your people. They will even go into your ovens and mixing bowls.
4 The frogs will come up on you, your people, and all your officials.'"
5 The LORD said to Moses, "Tell Aaron, 'Stretch out your hand with your staff. Hold it over the rivers, canals, and ponds. Make the frogs come up onto the land of Egypt.'"
6 So Aaron stretched out his hand over the waters of Egypt. The frogs came up and covered the land of Egypt.
7 But the magicians did the same thing with their magic tricks. They also brought frogs up onto the land of Egypt.
8 Pharaoh called Moses and Aaron. He said, "Pray to the LORD. Ask him to take the frogs away from me and my people. Then I will let your people go to sacrifice to the LORD."
9 Moses said to Pharaoh, "You may set the time. When shall I pray for you, your officials, and your people? When should the frogs leave you and your houses, and stay only in the Nile?"
10 "Tomorrow," Pharaoh answered. "May it be as you say," Moses replied. "Then you will know there is no one like the LORD our God.
11 The frogs will leave you, your houses, your officials, and your people. They will stay only in the Nile."
12 Moses and Aaron left Pharaoh. Then Moses cried out to the LORD about the frogs he had sent against Pharaoh.
13 The LORD did as Moses asked. The frogs in the houses, courtyards, and fields all died.
14 People piled them into big heaps. A terrible bad smell filled the land.
15 Pharaoh saw that the trouble was over. But he hardened his heart. He would not listen to Moses and Aaron, just as the LORD had said.
16 Then the LORD said to Moses, "Tell Aaron, 'Stretch out your staff and strike the dust of the earth. The dust will turn into swarms of gnats all over Egypt.'"
17 They did this. Aaron stretched out his hand with his staff and struck the dust. Gnats came on people and animals. All the dust of the earth turned into gnats throughout Egypt.
18 The magicians tried to make gnats with their magic tricks. But they could not. The gnats stayed on people and animals.
19 "This is the finger of God," the magicians said to Pharaoh. But Pharaoh's heart was hardened. He would not listen to them, just as the LORD had said.
20 Then the LORD said to Moses, "Get up early in the morning. Pharaoh will go out to the water. Stand before him and say, 'This is what the LORD says: Let my people go, so they can worship me.

21 If you will not let my people go, I will send swarms of flies. They will come on you, your officials, your people, and your houses. The houses of the Egyptians and even the ground will be full of flies.
22 But on that day I will deal differently with the land of Goshen, where my people live. No swarms of flies will be there. Then you will know that I, the LORD, am in the land.
23 I will make a difference between my people and your people. This sign will happen tomorrow.'"
24 The LORD did so. Thick swarms of flies poured into Pharaoh's palace and into the houses of his officials. Throughout Egypt, the land was ruined by swarms of flies.
25 Then Pharaoh called Moses and Aaron. He said, "Go, sacrifice to your God here in this land."
26 But Moses replied, "It would not be right to do that. The sacrifices we offer to the LORD our God are hated by the Egyptians. If they see us, will they not stone us?
27 We must take a three-day trip into the desert. We must sacrifice to the LORD our God, as he commands us."
28 Pharaoh answered, "I will let you go to sacrifice to the LORD your God in the desert. But you must not go very far. Now pray for me."

29 "As soon as I leave you," Moses said, "I will pray to the LORD. Tomorrow the swarms of flies will leave Pharaoh, his officials, and his people. But Pharaoh must not lie to us again. He must let the people go to sacrifice to the LORD."
30 Then Moses left Pharaoh and prayed to the LORD.
31 The LORD did as Moses asked. He took the swarms of flies away from Pharaoh, his officials, and his people. Not one fly was left.
32 But Pharaoh hardened his heart this time too. He would not let the people go.

title: Exodus 9
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Exodus
paragraphs: 1 Then the LORD said to Moses, "Go to Pharaoh and speak to him. Say this: 'The LORD, the God of the Hebrews, says: Let my people go, so they may worship me.
2 If you keep holding them back and refuse to let them go,
3 then the hand of the LORD will bring a terrible disease on your farm animals. It will strike your horses, donkeys, camels, herds, and flocks.
4 But the LORD will treat the livestock of Israel differently from the livestock of Egypt. No animal that belongs to the Israelites will die.'"
5 The LORD set a time. He said, "Tomorrow the LORD will do this in the land."
6 The next day the LORD did just that. All the livestock of the Egyptians died. But not one animal of the Israelites died.
7 Pharaoh sent officials to check. They found that no Israelite livestock had died. But Pharaoh's heart became hard. He would not let the people go.
8 Then the LORD said to Moses and Aaron, "Take handfuls of ash from the oven. Moses, throw it into the air in front of Pharaoh.
9 It will become fine dust over all the land of Egypt. Painful boils will break out on people and animals everywhere."
10 So they took ash from the oven and stood before Pharaoh. Moses threw it into the air. Painful boils broke out on people and animals.
11 The magicians could not stand before Moses. The boils had broken out on them and on all the Egyptians.
12 But the LORD made Pharaoh's heart hard. He would not listen to them, just as the LORD had said to Moses.
13 Then the LORD said to Moses, "Get up early in the morning. Stand before Pharaoh and tell him: 'The LORD, the God of the Hebrews, says: Let my people go, so they may worship me.
14 If not, I will send all my plagues against you, your officials, and your people. Then you will know that there is no one like me in all the earth.
15 By now I could have stretched out my hand. I could have struck you and your people with a plague to wipe you off the earth.
16 But I raised you up for this very purpose, that I might show you my power and that my name might be proclaimed in all the earth.
17 Still, you treat my people badly. You do not let them go.
18 Look — at this time tomorrow I will send down the worst hail. Egypt has never seen hail like this since it began.
19 So give orders now. Bring your livestock and everything you have in the field to shelter. Any person or animal left outside will die when the hail falls.'"
20 Some of Pharaoh's officials were afraid of the LORD's word. They hurried to bring their servants and livestock inside.
21 But others ignored the word of the LORD. They left their servants and livestock in the field.
22 Then the LORD said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand toward the sky. Then hail will fall on all the land of Egypt. It will fall on people, animals, and every plant in the field."
23 So Moses stretched out his staff toward the sky. The LORD sent thunder and hail. Lightning hit the earth. The LORD rained down hail on the land of Egypt.
24 The hail fell, and lightning kept flashing through it. The hail was so bad that nothing like it had ever been seen in Egypt before.
25 All through Egypt, the hail struck down everything in the field. It killed people and animals. It beat down every plant and stripped every tree.
26 The only place with no hail was the land of Goshen. That is where the Israelites lived.
27 Then Pharaoh called for Moses and Aaron. "This time I have sinned," he said. "The LORD is righteous. I and my people are in the wrong.
28 Pray to the LORD. There has been enough of God's thunder and hail. I will let you go. You do not need to stay any longer."
29 Moses said to him, "When I leave the city, I will spread out my hands to the LORD. The thunder will stop, and there will be no more hail. Then you will know that the earth belongs to the LORD.
30 But I know that you and your officials still do not fear the LORD our God."
31 (Now the flax and barley were destroyed. The barley was ripe, and the flax was in bloom.
32 But the wheat and spelt — a kind of wheat — were not destroyed. They grow later in the year.)
33 Then Moses left Pharaoh and went out of the city. He spread out his hands to the LORD. The thunder and hail stopped. The rain no longer poured down on the land.
34 When Pharaoh saw that the rain, hail, and thunder had stopped, he sinned again. He made his heart hard — he and his officials.
35 So Pharaoh's heart became hard. He would not let the Israelites go, just as the LORD had said through Moses.

title: Exodus 10
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Exodus
paragraphs: 1 Then the LORD said to Moses, "Go to Pharaoh. I have made his heart hard. I have also made the hearts of his officials hard. I want to show my wonderful signs among them.
2 I want you to tell your children and grandchildren. Tell them how harshly I dealt with the Egyptians. Tell them about the wonderful signs I did. Then you will all know that I am the LORD."

3 So Moses and Aaron went to Pharaoh. They told him, "This is what the LORD, the God of the Hebrews, says: 'How long will you refuse to bow down before Me? Let My people go, so that they may worship Me.
4 But if you refuse to let My people go, I will bring locusts into your land tomorrow.
5 They will cover the face of the land. No one will be able to see the ground. They will eat up whatever the hail left behind. They will eat every tree that grows in your fields.
6 They will fill your houses. They will fill the houses of all your officials and every Egyptian. Your fathers and grandfathers never saw such a thing.'" Then Moses turned and left Pharaoh.
7 Pharaoh's officials begged him, "How long will this man be a trap to us? Let the people go, so they may worship the LORD their God. Don't you see yet that Egypt is in ruins?"
8 So Moses and Aaron were brought back to Pharaoh. "Go, worship the LORD your God," he said. "But who exactly will be going?"
9 "We will all go," Moses replied. "We will go with our young and old. We will go with our sons and daughters. We will take our sheep and cattle. We must have a feast to the LORD."
10 Then Pharaoh told them, "May the LORD be with you, if I ever let you go with your little ones. Clearly you are planning evil.
11 No, only the men may go and worship the LORD. That is what you have been asking for." And Moses and Aaron were driven from Pharaoh.
12 Then the LORD said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand over the land of Egypt. Let the locusts come over it. They will eat every plant in the land. They will eat everything the hail has left behind."
13 So Moses stretched out his staff over the land of Egypt. All that day and night the LORD sent an east wind across the land. By morning the east wind had brought the locusts.
14 The locusts came over the whole land. They settled in all the territory of Egypt. There had never been so many locusts before. There will never be so many again.

15 They covered the face of the land until it was black. They ate up all the plants on the ground. They ate all the fruit on the trees that the hail had left. Nothing green was left on any tree or plant in all Egypt.
16 Pharaoh quickly called Moses and Aaron. He said, "I have sinned against the LORD your God and against you.
17 Now please forgive my sin once more. Pray to the LORD your God. Ask Him to take this deadly thing away from me."
18 So Moses left Pharaoh and prayed to the LORD.
19 And the LORD changed the wind to a very strong west wind. It carried off the locusts and blew them into the Red Sea. Not a single locust was left anywhere in Egypt.
20 But the LORD made Pharaoh's heart hard. He would not let the Israelites go.
21 Then the LORD said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand toward heaven. Then darkness will spread over the land of Egypt — a darkness that can be felt."
22 So Moses stretched out his hand toward heaven. Total darkness covered all the land of Egypt for three days.
23 No one could see anyone else. For three days no one moved from his place. Yet all the Israelites had light in the places where they lived.
24 Then Pharaoh called Moses and said, "Go, worship the LORD. Even your little ones may go with you. Only your sheep and cattle must stay behind."
25 But Moses replied, "You must also give us sacrifices and burnt offerings. We will present them to the LORD our God.
26 Our animals must also go with us. Not one animal foot will be left behind. We will need some of them to worship the LORD our God. We will not know how to worship the LORD until we arrive."
27 But the LORD made Pharaoh's heart hard. He was not willing to let them go.
28 "Get away from me!" Pharaoh said to Moses. "Make sure you never see my face again. On the day you see my face, you will die."
29 "As you say," Moses replied. "I will never see your face again."

title: Exodus 11
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Exodus
paragraphs: 1 Then the LORD said to Moses, "I will send one more plague on Pharaoh and Egypt. After that, he will let you leave. He will even drive you out completely."
2 "Now tell the people this. Every man and woman should ask their neighbors for things made of silver and gold."
3 The LORD made the people loved by the Egyptians. Moses himself was very respected in Egypt. Pharaoh's officials and the people honored him.
4 So Moses said, "This is what the LORD says: 'About midnight I will go throughout Egypt.

5 Every firstborn son in Egypt will die. The firstborn of Pharaoh who sits on his throne will die. The firstborn of the servant girl who grinds grain will die. Even the firstborn of all the cattle will die.
6 Then loud crying will go out over all Egypt. Such crying has never been heard before. It will never be heard again.
7 But among the Israelites, not a dog will bark at any person or animal.' Then you will know this. The LORD treats Egypt and Israel differently.
8 All your officials will come and bow down to me. They will say, 'Go, you and all the people who follow you!' After that, I will leave." Then Moses left Pharaoh, very angry.
9 The LORD said to Moses, "Pharaoh will not listen to you. So I may do more wonders in the land of Egypt."
10 Moses and Aaron did all these wonders before Pharaoh. But the LORD made Pharaoh's heart hard. So he would not let the Israelites leave his land.

title: Exodus 12
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Exodus
paragraphs: 1 The LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron in the land of Egypt. He said,
2 "This month is to be for you the first month, the first month of your year.
3 Tell all the people of Israel this. On the tenth day of this month, each man must choose a lamb. He must take one lamb for each family.
4 Some families are too small for a whole lamb. Then they must share with their nearest neighbor. They will count the people and divide the lamb.
5 Your lamb must be a lamb without defect. It must be a year-old male. You may take it from the sheep or the goats.
6 Take care of them until the fourteenth day of the month. Then all the people of Israel will slaughter them at twilight.
7 Take some of the blood and put it on the sides and tops of the doorframes of the houses. Do this where you eat the lambs.
8 Eat the meat roasted over the fire, along with bitter herbs, and bread made without yeast. Eat it that same night.
9 Do not eat the meat raw. Do not boil it in water. Roast it over the fire — head, legs, and inner parts.
10 Do not leave any of it until morning. Burn anything that is left over before morning.
11 This is how you must eat it: with your cloak tucked into your belt, your sandals on your feet and your staff in your hand. Eat it in haste; it is the LORD's Passover.
12 "On that night I will pass through the land of Egypt. I will strike down every firstborn male, both man and beast. I will judge all the gods of Egypt. I am the LORD.
13 The blood will be a sign for you on the houses where you are; and when I see the blood, I will pass over you. No destructive plague will touch you when I strike Egypt.
14 This is a day you are to commemorate; for the generations to come you shall celebrate it as a festival to the LORD — a lasting ordinance.
15 For seven days you must eat unleavened bread. On the first day, remove all yeast from your houses. Anyone who eats yeast from day one through day seven must be cut off from Israel.
16 On the first day, hold a sacred meeting. Hold another one on the seventh day. Do no work on those days. You may only prepare your food.
17 So keep the Feast of Unleavened Bread. On this very day I brought your people out of Egypt. Keep this day as a lasting ordinance for all generations.
18 In the first month, eat unleavened bread. Start on the evening of the fourteenth day. Stop on the evening of the twenty-first day.
19 For seven days, no yeast must be found in your houses. Anyone who eats yeast will be cut off from Israel. This applies to foreigners and native-born alike.
20 You must not eat anything with yeast. Eat unleavened bread in all your homes."
21 Then Moses called all the elders of Israel together. He told them, "Go at once and choose a lamb for each family. Slaughter the Passover lamb.
22 Take a bunch of hyssop. Dip it into the blood in the basin. Brush the blood on the top and sides of the doorframe. No one may go out the door of his house until morning.

23 The LORD will pass through to strike down the Egyptians. He will see the blood on the top and sides of the doorframe. He will pass over that doorway. He will not let the destroyer enter your houses to strike you.
24 Keep this command as a lasting ordinance for you and your children.
25 You will enter the land that the LORD will give you, as he promised. There you must keep this service.
26 Your children will ask you, 'What does this service mean to you?'

27 Then say to them, 'It is the Passover sacrifice to the LORD. He passed over the houses of the Israelites in Egypt. He struck down the Egyptians but spared our homes.'" Then the people bowed down and worshiped.
28 The Israelites went and did just what the LORD had commanded Moses and Aaron.

29 At midnight the LORD struck down every firstborn male in Egypt. He struck the firstborn of Pharaoh on his throne. He struck the firstborn of the prisoner in the dungeon. He also struck the firstborn of all the livestock.
30 Pharaoh got up in the night. All his officials and all the Egyptians got up too. There was loud wailing in Egypt. Every house had someone dead.
31 Pharaoh called for Moses and Aaron that night. He said, "Get up, leave my people! Both you and the Israelites — go! Worship the LORD as you have asked.
32 Take your flocks and herds, just as you said. Go! And bless me also."
33 The Egyptians urged the people to leave quickly. They said, "Otherwise, we are all going to die!"
34 So the people took their dough before yeast was added. They wrapped the bowls in their clothes and carried them on their shoulders.
35 The Israelites did what Moses had told them. They asked the Egyptians for silver, gold, and clothing.
36 The LORD made the Egyptians kind to the people. So the Egyptians gave them what they asked for. In this way they plundered the Egyptians.
37 The Israelites traveled from Rameses to Succoth. There were about 600,000 men on foot, besides women and children.
38 A mixed crowd also went up with them. They had large herds of livestock — flocks and herds.
39 Their dough had no yeast in it. So they baked what they had brought out of Egypt into flat loaves. They had been driven out and could not wait. They had not even prepared food for the journey.
40 The Israelites lived in Egypt for 430 years.
41 At the end of the 430 years, on the very same day, all the LORD's people went out of Egypt.
42 The LORD watched over them that night to bring them out of Egypt. So this same night belongs to the LORD. All the Israelites must keep it for all generations.
43 The LORD said to Moses and Aaron, "This is the rule of the Passover. No foreigner may eat it.
44 Any slave who has been bought may eat it, after you have circumcised him.
45 A temporary resident or hired worker may not eat the Passover.
46 Eat it inside one house. Do not take any of the meat outside the house. Do not break any of the bones.
47 The whole community of Israel must celebrate it.

48 A foreigner may live among you and want to celebrate the LORD's Passover. Then all the males in his house must be circumcised. After that, he may come and celebrate it. He will be like a native of the land. But no uncircumcised man may eat it.
49 The same law applies to the native and to the foreigner who lives among you."
50 All the Israelites did this. They did just as the LORD had commanded Moses and Aaron.
51 On that very day the LORD brought the Israelites out of the land of Egypt by their divisions.

title: Exodus 13
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Exodus
paragraphs: 1 Then the LORD said to Moses,
2 "Set apart for me every firstborn male. Consecrate to me every first child that opens the womb among the Israelites. This is true for both people and animals. They all belong to me."

3 So Moses told the people, "Remember this day. This is the day you came out of Egypt, out of the house of slavery. The LORD brought you out with his mighty hand. You must not eat any bread made with yeast.
4 Today, in the month of Abib, you are leaving.

5 The LORD will bring you into the land of the Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Hivites, and Jebusites. He promised your fathers to give you this land. It is a land full of milk and honey. There you must keep this festival in this month.
6 For seven days you must eat bread without yeast. On the seventh day, hold a feast to the LORD.
7 Eat bread without yeast for those seven days. No bread with yeast may be found among you. No yeast may be found anywhere in your land.
8 On that day, explain to your son, 'I do this because of what the LORD did for me when I came out of Egypt.'
9 Tie them as a sign on your hand and bind them on your forehead. This will remind you that the Law of the LORD must be on your lips. For with his mighty hand the LORD brought you out of Egypt.
10 So you must keep this rule at the right time every year.
11 The LORD will bring you into the land of the Canaanites. He will give it to you, as he promised you and your fathers.
12 Then you must give to the LORD every firstborn male that opens the womb. All the firstborn males of your animals belong to the LORD.
13 You must redeem every firstborn donkey with a lamb. If you do not redeem it, you must break its neck. You must also redeem every firstborn of your sons.
14 In the future, your son will ask you, 'What does this mean?' You must tell him, 'With his mighty hand the LORD brought us out of Egypt, out of the house of slavery.

15 Pharaoh refused stubbornly to let us go. So the LORD killed every firstborn in the land of Egypt. He killed both people and animals. That is why I sacrifice to the LORD every firstborn male that opens the womb. But I redeem all the firstborn of my sons.'
16 So tie them as a sign on your hand and bind them on your forehead. For with his mighty hand the LORD brought us out of Egypt."

17 Pharaoh let the people go. But God did not lead them along the road through the land of the Philistines. That road was shorter. God said, "If the people face war, they might change their minds. Then they would return to Egypt."
18 So God led the people around by the way of the wilderness toward the Red Sea. The Israelites left the land of Egypt ready for battle.

19 Moses took the bones of Joseph with him. Long ago, Joseph had made the sons of Israel take a serious promise. He said, "God will surely take care of you. Then you must carry my bones with you from this place."
20 They set out from Succoth. They camped at Etham on the edge of the wilderness.
21 The LORD went before them in a pillar of cloud by day to guide their way. He went in a pillar of fire by night to give them light. So they could travel by day or night.
22 Neither the pillar of cloud by day nor the pillar of fire by night left its place before the people.

title: Exodus 14
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Exodus
paragraphs: 1 Then the LORD said to Moses,
2 "Tell the Israelites to turn back and camp near Pi-hahiroth. Camp between Migdol and the sea, across from Baal-zephon.
3 Pharaoh will say, 'The Israelites are lost in the land. The desert has trapped them.'
4 I will make Pharaoh's heart stubborn. He will chase after them. But I will be honored through Pharaoh and his army. Then the Egyptians will know that I am the LORD." So the Israelites did this.
5 Someone told the king of Egypt that the people had run away. Pharaoh and his officials changed their minds. They said, "What have we done? We have let Israel go from our service!"
6 So Pharaoh got his chariot ready and took his army with him.
7 He took 600 of the best chariots and all the other chariots of Egypt. Officers led every one of them.
8 The LORD made the heart of Pharaoh, king of Egypt, stubborn. So he chased the Israelites, who were marching out with their heads high.
9 The Egyptians chased them with all of Pharaoh's horses, chariots, horsemen, and troops. They caught up with them by the sea near Pi-hahiroth, across from Baal-zephon.
10 As Pharaoh came near, the Israelites looked up. They saw the Egyptians marching after them. They were very afraid and cried out to the LORD.
11 They said to Moses, "Were there no graves in Egypt? Did you bring us to the desert to die? What have you done to us by leading us out of Egypt?
12 Did we not say to you in Egypt, 'Leave us alone. Let us serve the Egyptians'? It would have been better to serve them than to die in the desert!"
13 But Moses told the people, "Do not be afraid. Stand strong and you will see how the LORD will save you today. The Egyptians you see today, you will never see again.
14 The LORD will fight for you; you need only to be still."
15 Then the LORD said to Moses, "Why are you crying out to me? Tell the Israelites to move forward.
16 Lift up your staff. Stretch out your hand over the sea and divide it. Then the Israelites can go through the sea on dry ground.
17 I will make the hearts of the Egyptians stubborn. They will go in after them. And I will be honored through Pharaoh, his army, his chariots, and his horsemen.
18 The Egyptians will know that I am the LORD. They will know it when I am honored through Pharaoh, his chariots, and his horsemen."
19 The angel of God had gone before the camp of Israel. Now he moved and went behind them. The pillar of cloud also moved from in front of them. It stood behind them.
20 So it came between the camps of Egypt and Israel. The cloud was dark on one side, but it lit up the night. All night long, neither camp went near the other.
21 Moses stretched out his hand over the sea, and all that night the LORD drove the sea back with a strong east wind and turned it into dry land. The waters were divided.
22 The Israelites went through the sea on dry ground, with a wall of water on their right and on their left.
23 The Egyptians chased after them. All of Pharaoh's horses, chariots, and horsemen followed them into the sea.
24 In the early morning, the LORD looked down on the Egyptian army. He looked from the pillar of fire and cloud. He filled their camp with panic.
25 He made their chariot wheels shake, so it was hard to drive. The Egyptians said, "Let us run from the Israelites! The LORD is fighting for them against Egypt!"
26 Then the LORD said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand over the sea. The waters will flow back over the Egyptians, their chariots, and their horsemen."
27 So Moses stretched out his hand over the sea. At sunrise, the sea returned to normal. As the Egyptians were running away, the LORD washed them into the sea.
28 The waters flowed back and covered the chariots and horsemen. The whole army of Pharaoh had chased Israel into the sea. Not even one of them lived.
29 But the Israelites had walked through the sea on dry ground. There was a wall of water on their right and on their left.
30 That day the LORD saved Israel from the hands of the Egyptians, and Israel saw the Egyptians lying dead on the shore.
31 And when the Israelites saw the mighty hand of the LORD displayed against the Egyptians, the people feared the LORD and put their trust in him and in Moses his servant.

title: Exodus 15
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Exodus
paragraphs: 1 Then Moses and the Israelites sang this song to the LORD: "I will sing to the LORD, for he is highly exalted. The horse and its rider he has hurled into the sea.
2 The LORD is my strength and my song; he has become my salvation. He is my God, and I will praise him, my father's God, and I will lift him high.
3 The LORD is a warrior; the LORD is his name.
4 Pharaoh's chariots and army he has thrown into the sea; the best of his officers drowned in the Red Sea.
5 The deep waters covered them; they sank down like a stone.
6 Your right hand, O LORD, was majestic in power; your right hand, O LORD, broke the enemy in pieces.
7 With your great power you threw down those who rose against you. You sent out your burning anger; it burned them up like dry grass.
8 At the blast of your breath the waters piled up; the currents stood firm like a wall; the deep waters hardened in the heart of the sea.
9 The enemy said, 'I will chase them. I will catch them. I will share out what I take. I will take my fill. I will draw my sword; my hand will destroy them.'
10 But you blew with your breath, and the sea covered them. They sank like lead in the mighty waters.
11 Who among the gods is like you, O LORD? Who is like you — majestic in holiness, praised with awe, doing wonders?
12 You stretched out your right hand, and the earth swallowed them up.
13 With your faithful love you will lead the people you have set free; with your strength you will guide them to your holy home.
14 The nations will hear and shake with fear; great pain will grip the people of Philistia.
15 Then the chiefs of Edom will be afraid; trembling will seize the leaders of Moab; the people who live in Canaan will melt away.
16 Terror and fear will fall on them. By the power of your arm they will be still as a stone, until your people pass by, O LORD, until the people you have bought pass by.
17 You will bring them in and plant them on the mountain of your inheritance — the place, O LORD, you have prepared for your home, the holy place, O Lord, your hands have made firm.
18 The LORD reigns for ever and ever!"
19 When Pharaoh's horses, chariots, and horsemen went into the sea, the LORD brought the waters back over them. But the Israelites walked through the sea on dry ground.
20 Then Miriam the prophet, Aaron's sister, took a tambourine in her hand. All the women followed her with tambourines and dancing.
21 And Miriam sang back to them: "Sing to the LORD, for he is highly exalted; the horse and its rider he has hurled into the sea."
22 Then Moses led Israel away from the Red Sea. They went out into the Desert of Shur. For three days they walked in the desert and found no water.
23 When they came to Marah, they could not drink the water there. It was bitter. (That is why the place was named Marah.)
24 So the people complained against Moses. They said, "What are we to drink?"
25 Moses cried out to the LORD. And the LORD showed him a piece of wood. He threw the wood into the water, and the water became sweet. There the LORD gave them a rule and a law. There he tested them.

26 He said, "Listen carefully to the voice of the LORD your God. Do what is right in his eyes. Pay attention to his commands. Keep all his rules. Then I will not put on you any of the diseases I put on the Egyptians. For I am the LORD, who heals you."
27 Then they came to Elim. There were twelve springs of water and seventy palm trees there. So they camped there by the waters.

title: Exodus 16
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Exodus
paragraphs: 1 It was the fifteenth day of the second month after they left Egypt. All the people of Israel set out from Elim. They came to the Desert of Sin, between Elim and Sinai.
2 There in the desert, they all complained against Moses and Aaron.

3 They said, "If only we had died by the LORD's hand in Egypt! There we sat by pots of meat. We ate all the bread we wanted. But you have brought us into this desert. You want to starve us all to death!"

4 Then the LORD said to Moses, "Look, I will rain down bread from heaven for you. Each day the people must go out. They will gather enough for that day. In this way I will test them. I want to see if they will follow my rules.
5 On the sixth day, they will prepare what they bring in. It will be twice as much as they gather on other days."
6 So Moses and Aaron said to all the Israelites, "This evening you will know something. The LORD himself brought you out of Egypt.
7 And in the morning you will see the LORD's glory. He has heard you complain against him. Who are we? Why do you complain against us?"

8 Moses added, "The LORD will give you meat to eat this evening. And in the morning he will give you all the bread you want. He has heard you complain against him. Who are we? Your complaints are not against us, but against the LORD."
9 Then Moses said to Aaron, "Tell all the people of Israel, 'Come before the LORD. He has heard your complaining.'"
10 While Aaron was speaking to all the people, they looked toward the desert. And there in a cloud, the glory of the LORD appeared.
11 Then the LORD said to Moses,

12 "I have heard the complaining of the Israelites. Tell them, 'In the evening you will eat meat. And in the morning you will have all the bread you want. Then you will know that I am the LORD your God.'"
13 That evening, quail came and covered the camp. And in the morning, there was a layer of dew around the camp.
14 When the dew dried up, thin flakes were on the desert floor. They were as fine as frost on the ground.
15 When the Israelites saw it, they asked each other, "What is it?" They did not know what it was. So Moses told them, "It is the bread that the LORD has given you to eat.
16 This is what the LORD has commanded: 'Each person is to gather as much as he needs. Take an omer for each person in your tent.'"
17 So the Israelites did this. Some gathered more, and some less.
18 When they measured it by the omer, it was just right. The one who gathered much had nothing extra. The one who gathered little had enough. Each person had as much as he needed to eat.
19 Then Moses said to them, "No one may keep any of it until morning."
20 But they did not listen to Moses. Some people left part of it until morning. It became full of worms and began to smell. So Moses was angry with them.
21 Every morning, each person gathered as much as he needed. When the sun grew hot, it melted away.
22 On the sixth day, they gathered twice as much food — two omers per person. All the leaders of the people came and told Moses.

23 He told them, "This is what the LORD has said: 'Tomorrow is a day of full rest, a holy Sabbath to the LORD. So bake what you want to bake. Boil what you want to boil. Then set aside what is left. Keep it until morning.'"
24 So they set it aside until morning, as Moses had commanded. It did not smell, and it had no worms.
25 "Eat it today," Moses said, "because today is a Sabbath to the LORD. Today you will not find any in the field.
26 For six days you may gather. But on the seventh day, the Sabbath, there will be none."
27 Yet on the seventh day, some of the people went out to gather. But they did not find anything.
28 Then the LORD said to Moses, "How long will you refuse to keep my commands and rules?

29 Understand this: the LORD has given you the Sabbath. That is why on the sixth day he gives you bread for two days. On the seventh day, everyone must stay where he is. No one is to go out from his place."
30 So the people rested on the seventh day.
31 The house of Israel called the bread manna. It was white like coriander seed. It tasted like thin cakes made with honey.

32 Moses said, "This is what the LORD has commanded: 'Keep an omer of manna for the people of all future years. Then they may see the bread I fed you in the desert. I gave it to you when I brought you out of Egypt.'"
33 So Moses told Aaron, "Take a jar and fill it with an omer of manna. Then place it before the LORD. Keep it for the people of all future years."
34 So Aaron placed it in front of the Testimony, to be kept safe. He did just as the LORD had commanded Moses.
35 The Israelites ate manna for forty years. They ate it until they came to a land where they could settle. They ate manna until they reached the border of Canaan.
36 (Now an omer is a tenth of a large measure.)

title: Exodus 17
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Exodus
paragraphs: 1 The whole group of Israel left the Desert of Sin. They moved from place to place as the LORD commanded. They camped at Rephidim, but there was no water to drink.
2 So the people argued with Moses. They said, "Give us water to drink." Moses replied, "Why do you argue with me? Why do you test the LORD?"
3 But the people were very thirsty there. They complained against Moses. "Why did you bring us out of Egypt? Do you want us, our children, and our animals to die of thirst?"
4 Then Moses cried out to the LORD. "What should I do with these people? Soon they will throw stones at me!"
5 The LORD said to Moses, "Walk ahead of the people. Take some of the elders of Israel with you. Take in your hand the staff that you used to hit the Nile. Then go.
6 Look, I will stand there in front of you by the rock at Horeb. Strike the rock, and water will come out of it for the people to drink." So Moses did this in front of the elders of Israel.
7 He named the place Massah ("testing") and Meribah ("quarreling"). The Israelites had argued there. They had tested the LORD by saying, "Is the LORD among us or not?"
8 After this, the Amalekites came and attacked the Israelites at Rephidim.
9 So Moses said to Joshua, "Choose some of our men. Go out and fight the Amalekites. Tomorrow I will stand on the hilltop with the staff of God in my hand."
10 Joshua did as Moses told him and fought the Amalekites. Moses, Aaron, and Hur went up to the top of the hill.
11 As long as Moses held up his hands, Israel was winning. But when he lowered them, Amalek was winning.
12 Moses' hands grew tired. So they took a stone and put it under him. He sat on it. Then Aaron and Hur held his hands up, one on each side. His hands stayed steady until the sun went down.
13 So Joshua defeated Amalek and his army with the sword.
14 Then the LORD said to Moses, "Write this on a scroll as a reminder. Read it out to Joshua. I will completely wipe out the memory of Amalek from under heaven."
15 Moses built an altar there. He named it "The LORD Is My Banner" (Jehovah-Nissi).
16 He said, "A hand was lifted up to the throne of the LORD. The LORD will be at war against the Amalekites from generation to generation."

title: Exodus 18
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Exodus
paragraphs: 1 Moses' father-in-law Jethro was the priest of Midian. He heard all that God had done for Moses and Israel. He heard how the LORD had brought Israel out of Egypt.
2 Earlier, Moses had sent his wife Zipporah back to her father. So Jethro had taken her in.
3 He also took in her two sons. One son was named Gershom. Moses had said, "I have been a foreigner in a foreign land."
4 The other son was named Eliezer. Moses had said, "The God of my father was my helper. He saved me from the sword of Pharaoh."
5 Jethro came to Moses in the desert. He brought Moses' wife and sons. Moses was camped at the mountain of God.
6 Jethro sent word to Moses. He said, "I, your father-in-law Jethro, am coming to you. Your wife and her two sons are with me."
7 So Moses went out to meet his father-in-law. He bowed down and kissed him. They greeted each other and went into the tent.

8 Then Moses told his father-in-law everything. He spoke of all the LORD had done to Pharaoh and the Egyptians for Israel. He told of all the hard times on the way. And he told how the LORD had saved them.
9 Jethro was glad about all the good things the LORD had done for Israel. The LORD had rescued them from the hand of the Egyptians.
10 Jethro said, "Praise be to the LORD! He has saved you from the hand of the Egyptians and Pharaoh. He has saved the people from the hand of the Egyptians.
11 Now I know that the LORD is greater than all other gods. He did this when they treated Israel with pride."
12 Then Jethro brought a burnt offering and sacrifices to God. Aaron came with all the elders of Israel. They ate bread with Moses' father-in-law before God.
13 The next day Moses sat to judge the people. They stood around him from morning until evening.
14 Jethro saw all that Moses was doing for the people. He asked, "What is this that you are doing? Why do you sit alone as judge? Why do all these people stand around you from morning till evening?"
15 Moses replied, "Because the people come to me to ask God.
16 Whenever they have an argument, they bring it to me. I judge between one man and another. And I teach them the laws and rules of God."
17 But his father-in-law said to him, "What you are doing is not good.
18 You and these people will surely wear yourselves out. The work is too heavy for you. You cannot do it alone.
19 Now listen to me. I will give you some advice, and may God be with you. You must be the people's voice before God. Bring their cases to him.
20 Teach them the laws and rules. Show them the way to live and the work they must do.
21 Also, choose capable men from among the people. Pick men who fear God, trustworthy men who hate dishonest gain. Appoint them as officials over thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens.

22 Have these men judge the people at all times. They can bring any big case to you. But they can judge the small cases on their own. In this way, your load will be lighter, and they will share it with you.
23 If you follow this advice and God so directs you, then you will be able to keep going. And all these people can go home in peace."
24 Moses listened to his father-in-law and did everything he said.
25 So Moses chose capable men from all Israel. He made them heads over the people. They became officials over thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens.
26 They judged the people at all times. They would bring the hard cases to Moses. But any small case they would judge themselves.
27 Then Moses said goodbye to his father-in-law. And Jethro returned to his own land.

title: Exodus 19
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Exodus
paragraphs: 1 In the third month after Israel left Egypt, they came to the Desert of Sinai. It was the same day of the month.
2 They had left Rephidim and entered the Desert of Sinai. Israel camped there in front of the mountain.
3 Then Moses went up to God. The LORD called to him from the mountain. He said, "Tell the house of Jacob and the sons of Israel:
4 'You yourselves have seen what I did to Egypt. You saw how I carried you on eagles' wings and brought you to myself.
5 Now if you obey my voice and keep my covenant, you will be my treasured possession out of all nations. The whole earth is mine.
6 You will be for me a kingdom of priests and a holy nation.' These are the words you must speak to the Israelites."
7 So Moses went back. He called the elders of the people together. He set before them all the words the LORD had commanded.
8 All the people answered together, "We will do everything the LORD has spoken." So Moses brought their words back to the LORD.
9 The LORD said to Moses, "Look, I will come to you in a thick cloud. The people will hear when I speak with you. Then they will always trust you." And Moses told the LORD what the people had said.
10 Then the LORD said to Moses, "Go to the people. Make them holy today and tomorrow. They must wash their clothes.
11 Get them ready by the third day. On the third day the LORD will come down on Mount Sinai. All the people will see it.
12 Set up a boundary for the people around the mountain. Tell them, 'Be careful. Do not go up on the mountain. Do not touch its base. Anyone who touches the mountain must die.
13 No hand shall touch him. He must be stoned or shot with arrows. Whether man or animal, he must not live.' They may come near the mountain only when the ram's horn sounds a long blast."
14 Moses came down from the mountain to the people. He made them holy, and they washed their clothes.
15 He said to the people, "Get ready for the third day. Do not come close to a woman."
16 On the third day, when morning came, there was thunder and lightning. A thick cloud was on the mountain. A very loud blast of the ram's horn went out. All the people in the camp trembled.
17 Then Moses brought the people out of the camp to meet with God. They stood at the foot of the mountain.
18 Mount Sinai was completely covered with smoke. The LORD had come down on it in fire. The smoke rose like the smoke of a furnace. The whole mountain shook hard.
19 The sound of the ram's horn grew louder and louder. Moses spoke, and God answered him in the thunder.
20 The LORD came down to the top of Mount Sinai. He called Moses to the top. So Moses went up.
21 The LORD said to him, "Go down and warn the people. They must not push past to see the LORD. If they do, many of them will die.
22 Even the priests who come close to the LORD must make themselves holy. If not, the LORD will break out against them."
23 But Moses said to the LORD, "The people cannot come up Mount Sinai. You warned us yourself. You said, 'Put a boundary around the mountain and set it apart as holy.'"
24 The LORD replied, "Go down and bring Aaron with you. But the priests and the people must not push past to come up to the LORD. If they do, he will break out against them."
25 So Moses went down to the people and spoke to them.

title: Exodus 20
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Exodus
paragraphs: 1 And God spoke all these words.
2 "I am the LORD your God. I brought you out of Egypt, out of the land of slavery.
3 You shall have no other gods before me.
4 You shall not make for yourself an image. Do not make an image of anything in the sky above, on the earth below, or in the waters under the earth.

5 You shall not bow down to them or worship them. For I, the LORD your God, am a jealous God. I punish the children for the sin of the fathers, to the third and fourth generation of those who hate me.
6 But I show love to a thousand generations of those who love me and keep my commandments.
7 You shall not misuse the name of the LORD your God. The LORD will not leave anyone unpunished who misuses his name.
8 Remember the Sabbath day by keeping it holy.
9 Six days you shall labor and do all your work.

10 But the seventh day is a Sabbath to the LORD your God. On that day you must not do any work. This applies to you, your son or daughter, your male or female servant, your farm animals, and the foreigner who lives in your town.
11 For in six days the LORD made the sky, the earth, the sea, and all that is in them. But on the seventh day he rested. So the LORD blessed the Sabbath day and set it apart as holy.
12 Honor your father and your mother. Then you will live long in the land the LORD your God is giving you.
13 You shall not murder.
14 You shall not commit adultery.
15 You shall not steal.
16 You shall not give false testimony against your neighbor.
17 You shall not covet your neighbor's house. You shall not covet your neighbor's wife, or his male or female servant, his ox or donkey, or anything that belongs to your neighbor."
18 The people saw the thunder and lightning. They heard the trumpet. They saw the mountain covered in smoke. They shook with fear and stood far away.
19 They said to Moses, "Speak to us yourself and we will listen. But do not have God speak to us, or we will die."
20 Moses replied, "Do not be afraid. God has come to test you. He wants the fear of God to be with you, to keep you from sinning."
21 The people stood far away. But Moses came close to the thick darkness where God was.
22 Then the LORD said to Moses, "Tell the Israelites this: 'You have seen for yourselves that I spoke to you from heaven.
23 Do not make any gods to be alongside me. Do not make for yourselves gods of silver or gods of gold.

24 Make for me an altar of earth. On it, sacrifice your burnt offerings and peace offerings, your sheep, goats, and cattle. In every place where I cause my name to be remembered, I will come to you and bless you.
25 If you make an altar of stones for me, do not build it with stones cut by tools. If you use a chisel on a stone, you will make it unclean.
26 Do not go up to my altar on steps, so that your body will not be uncovered on it.'"

title: Exodus 21
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Exodus
paragraphs: 1 "These are the laws you must give to the people:
2 If you buy a Hebrew servant, he must serve you for six years. But in the seventh year, he shall go free. He pays nothing.
3 If he came alone, he shall leave alone. If he came with a wife, she shall leave with him.
4 If his master gives him a wife and she has sons or daughters, the wife and her children belong to the master. Only the man shall go free.
5 But the servant may say, 'I love my master and my wife and children. I do not want to go free.'
6 Then his master shall bring him before the judges. He shall take him to the door or doorpost. He shall push a sharp tool through his ear. Then the servant shall serve his master for life.
7 If a man sells his daughter as a servant, she shall not go free like the male servants.
8 Her master may have chosen her for himself but is not pleased with her. Then he must let her be bought back. He has no right to sell her to foreigners. He has not been fair to her.
9 If he chooses her for his son, he must treat her like a daughter.
10 If he takes another wife, he must not give the first wife less food or clothing. He must not take away her rights as his wife.
11 If he does not give her these three things, she may go free. She pays no money.
12 Whoever strikes and kills a person must surely be put to death.
13 But if he did not plan it, and God allowed it to happen, I will choose a place where he can run for safety.
14 But if anyone plans an attack on purpose and kills his neighbor, you must take him away from my altar. He must be put to death.
15 Whoever strikes his father or mother must surely be put to death.
16 Whoever kidnaps another person must be put to death. This is true whether he sells the person or still has him.
17 Whoever speaks evil against his father or mother must surely be put to death.
18 Men may fight, and one may strike the other with a stone or a fist. The man does not die but must stay in bed.

19 Then the one who struck him shall not be punished. The other man must be able to get up and walk outside with his stick. But he must pay back the lost work. He must also make sure the man is fully healed.
20 If a man strikes his male or female servant with a stick, and the servant dies, he must surely be punished.
21 But if the servant gets up after a day or two, the owner shall not be punished. The servant is his property.
22 Men may be fighting and strike a pregnant woman. Her child may be born too early but with no other harm. Then the man must pay a fine. The woman's husband sets the amount, and the court agrees.
23 But if there is serious harm, then you must require a life for a life —
24 eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot,
25 burn for burn, wound for wound, bruise for bruise.
26 If a man strikes and blinds the eye of his male or female servant, he must let the servant go free. This pays for the eye.
27 If he knocks out the tooth of his male or female servant, he must let the servant go free. This pays for the tooth.
28 If an ox gores a man or woman to death, the ox must be stoned to death. Its meat must not be eaten. But the owner of the ox shall not be punished.

29 But the ox may have a habit of goring. Its owner may have been warned but did not keep it under control. If it kills a man or woman, then the ox must be stoned. And its owner must also be put to death.
30 If payment is demanded of him instead, he may save his life. He must pay the full amount demanded.
31 If the ox gores a son or a daughter, the same rule applies.
32 If the ox gores a male or female servant, the owner must pay thirty shekels of silver to the servant's master. The ox must be stoned.
33 A man may open or dig a pit and fail to cover it. An ox or a donkey may fall into it.
34 Then the owner of the pit must pay back the loss. He must pay the animal's owner. The dead animal will be his.
35 If one man's ox hurts his neighbor's ox and it dies, they must sell the live one. They must share the money. They must also share the dead animal.
36 But the ox may have had a habit of goring, and its owner did not keep it under control. Then he must pay full payment, ox for ox. The dead animal will be his.

title: Exodus 22
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Exodus
paragraphs: 1 "If a man steals an ox or a sheep, he must pay back. He must give five oxen for one ox. He must give four sheep for one sheep. This is true if he killed it or sold it.
2 If a thief is caught breaking into a house at night, no one is guilty if he dies.
3 But if it happens after sunrise, the one who killed him is guilty. A thief must pay back in full. If he has nothing, he must be sold to pay for his theft.
4 Maybe the stolen animal is still alive with him — an ox, a donkey, or a sheep. Then he must pay back double.
5 A man may let his farm animals eat in a field or vineyard. If they stray into someone else's field, he must pay back. He must give the best of his own field or vineyard.
6 A fire may break out and spread to thornbushes. It may burn stacked grain, standing grain, or the whole field. The one who started the fire must pay back in full.
7 A man may give his neighbor money or goods to keep safe. If they are stolen from the neighbor's house, and the thief is caught, the thief must pay back double.
8 If the thief is not found, the owner of the house must go to the judges. They will decide if he took his neighbor's property.

9 Someone may claim, "This is mine" — about an ox, donkey, sheep, coat, or any lost item. Both men must bring their case to the judges. The one the judges find guilty must pay back double to his neighbor.
10 A man may give a donkey, ox, sheep, or any animal to his neighbor to care for. The animal may die, get hurt, or be stolen while no one is watching.
11 Then an oath before the LORD must be made between them. It will show if he took his neighbor's property. The owner must accept the oath. No payment is needed.
12 But if the animal was really stolen, the neighbor must pay back the owner.
13 If wild animals tore it to pieces, he must bring the body as proof. Then he does not need to pay back.
14 A man may borrow an animal from his neighbor. If it gets hurt or dies while the owner is not there, he must pay back in full.
15 If the owner was there, no payment is needed. If the animal was rented, the rental fee covers the loss.
16 A man may tempt an unmarried young woman and sleep with her. He must pay the full bride-price for her to be his wife.
17 If her father refuses to give her to him, the man must still pay. The amount must be like the bride-price for a virgin.
18 You must not allow a sorceress to live.
19 Anyone who has sex with an animal must be put to death.
20 Anyone who offers sacrifices to other gods must be set apart for destruction. Only the LORD is to be worshiped.
21 You must not mistreat or oppress a foreigner. Remember — you yourselves were foreigners in the land of Egypt.
22 You must not harm any widow or orphan.
23 If you harm them, and they cry out to me in pain, I will surely hear their cry.
24 My anger will burn hot. I will kill you with the sword. Then your wives will be widows, and your children will have no father.
25 You may lend money to one of my people who is poor. Do not act like a money-lender to him. Do not charge him interest.
26 You may take your neighbor's cloak as security. But you must give it back to him by sunset.
